problem_statement:
{"contestId": 2102, "index": "A", "name": "Dinner Time", "statement": "\begin{problem}{Dinner Time}{standard input}{standard output}{1 second}{256 megabytes}

\NewDocumentCommand{\DefTestcases}{O{t}O{10^4}}{Each test contains multiple test cases.
The first line contains the number of test cases $#1$ ($1 \le #1 \le #2$).
The description of the test cases follows.
}

Given four integers $n$, $m$, $p$, and $q$, determine whether there exists an integer array $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ (elements may be negative) satisfying the following conditions:

\begin{itemize}
\item The sum of all elements in the array is equal to $m$:
$$a_1 + a_2 + \ldots + a_n = m$$
\item The sum of every $p$ consecutive elements is equal to $q$:
$$a_i + a_{i + 1} + \ldots + a_{i + p - 1} = q,\qquad\text{ for all }1\le i\le n-p+1$$
\end{itemize}

\InputFile
\DefTestcases[t][10^4]

The first and only line of each test case contains four integers $n$, $m$, $p$, and $q$ ($1 \le p \le n \le 100$, $1 \le q, m \le 100$)~--- the length of the array, the sum of elements, the length of a segment, and the sum of a segment, respectively.

\OutputFile
For each test case, output ``\t{YES}'' (without quotes) if there exists an array satisfying the above conditions, and ``\t{NO}'' (without quotes) otherwise.

You can output ``\t{YES}'' and ``\t{NO}'' in any case (for example, strings ``\t{yES}'', ``\t{yes}'', and ``\t{Yes}'' will all be recognized as valid responses).

\Example

\begin{example}
\exmpfile{example.01}{example.01.a}%
\end{example}

\Note
In the first test case, an example of an array satisfying the condition is $[1, 0, 1]$. This is because:

\begin{itemize}
\item $a_1+a_2+a_3 = 1+0+1 = 2 = m$
\item $a_1+a_2=1+0=1=q$
\item $a_2+a_3=0+1=1=q$
\end{itemize}

In the second test case, the only array satisfying the condition is $[1]$.

In the third test case, an example of an array satisfying the condition is $[-2, 5, -2, 5, -2]$.

In the fourth test case, it can be proven that there is no array satisfying the condition.

\end{problem}

", "examples": {"example.01.a": "YES
YES
YES
NO
NO
", "example.01": "5
3 2 2 1
1 1 1 1
5 4 2 3
10 7 5 2
4 4 1 3
"}}

{"contestId": 2102, "index": "B", "name": "The Picky Cat", "statement": "\begin{problem}{The Picky Cat}{standard input}{standard output}{1 second}{256 megabytes}

\NewDocumentCommand{\DefTestcases}{O{t}O{10^4}}{Each test contains multiple test cases.
The first line contains the number of test cases $#1$ ($1 \le #1 \le #2$).
The description of the test cases follows.
}
\NewDocumentCommand{\DefLimitSum}{O{n}O{10^5}}{It is guaranteed that the sum of $#1$ over all test cases does not exceed $#2$.
}

You are given an array of integers $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$. You are allowed to do the following operation any number of times (possibly zero):

\begin{itemize}
\item Choose an index $i$ ($1\le i\le n$). Multiply $a_i$ by $-1$ (i.e., update $a_i := -a_i$).
\end{itemize}

Your task is to determine whether it is possible to make the element at index $1$ become the median of the array after doing the above operation any number of times. Note that operations can be applied to index $1$ as well, meaning the median can be either the original value of $a_1$ or its negation.

The median of an array $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m$ is defined as the $\left\lceil \frac{m}{2} \right\rceil$-th\footnote{$\lceil x \rceil$ is the ceiling function which returns the least integer greater than or equal to $x$.} smallest element of array $b$. For example, the median of the array $[3, 1, 2]$ is $2$, while the median of the array $[10, 1, 8, 3]$ is $3$.

It is guaranteed that the absolute value of the elements of $a$ are distinct. Formally, there are no pairs of indices $1\le i < j\le n$ where $|a_i| = |a_j|$.

\InputFile
\DefTestcases[t][10^4]

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $n$ ($1\le n\le 10^5$)~--- the length of the array $a$.

The second line of each test case contains $n$ integers $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$ ($|a_i|\le 10^6$, $|a_i|\neq |a_j|$)~--- the elements of the array $a$.

\DefLimitSum[n][10^5]

\OutputFile
For each testcase, output ``\t{YES}'' if it is possible to make the element at index $1$ become the median of the array, and ``\t{NO}'' otherwise.

You can output the answer in any case (upper or lower). For example, the strings ``\t{yEs}'', ``\t{yes}'', ``\t{Yes}'', and ``\t{YES}'' will be recognized as positive responses.

\Example

\begin{example}
\exmpfile{example.01}{example.01.a}%
\end{example}

\Note
In the first test case, $a_1 = 2$ is already the median of the array $a = [2, 3, 1]$, so no operation is required.

In the second test case, we can do two operations: one on index $2$, and one on index $5$. The array becomes $[1, -2, 3, 4, -5]$, which has a median of $1$.

In the third test case, if you do an operation on index $1$, the array will become $[-4, 2, 0, -5]$, which has a median of $-4$.

In the fourth test case, it can be proven that no sequence of operations can make the median of the array become $5$ or $-5$.

\end{problem}

", "examples": {"example.01.a": "YES
YES
YES
NO
NO
YES
YES
", "example.01": "7
3
2 3 1
5
1 2 3 4 5
4
4 2 0 -5
4
-5 0 4 3
4
-10 8 3 2
1
1
10
9 1000 -999 -13 456 -223 23 24 10 0
"}}

examples.example.01.a:
YES
YES
YES
NO
NO
YES
YES

problem_statement:
{"contestId": 2102, "index": "C", "name": "Mex in the Grid", "statement": "\begin{problem}{Mex in the Grid}{standard input}{standard output}{2 seconds}{256 megabytes}

\NewDocumentCommand{\DefLimitSum}{O{n}O{10^5}}{It is guaranteed that the sum of $#1$ over all test cases does not exceed $#2$.
}
\NewDocumentCommand{\DefTestcases}{O{t}O{10^4}}{Each test contains multiple test cases.
The first line contains the number of test cases $#1$ ($1 \le #1 \le #2$).
The description of the test cases follows.
}

You are given $n^2$ cards with values from $0$ to $n^2-1$. You are to arrange them in a $n$ by $n$ grid such that there is \textbf{exactly} one card in each cell.

The MEX (minimum excluded value) of a subgrid\footnote{A subgrid of a $n$ by $n$ grid is specified by four numbers $l_1, r_1, l_2, r_2$ satisfying $1\le l_1\le r_1\le n$ and $1\le l_2\le r_2\le n$. The element in the $i$-th row and the $j$-th column of the grid is part of the subgrid if and only if $l_1\le i\le r_1$ and $l_2\le j\le r_2$.} is defined as the smallest non-negative integer that does not appear in the subgrid.

Your task is to arrange the cards such that the sum of MEX values over all $\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2$ subgrids is maximized.

\InputFile
\DefTestcases[t][100]

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $n$ ($1\le n\le 500$)~--- the side length of the grid.

\DefLimitSum[n][1000]

\OutputFile
For each test case, output $n$ lines, each containing $n$ integers representing the elements of the grid. 

If there are multiple answers, you can output any of them.

\Example

\begin{example}
\exmpfile{example.01}{example.01.a}%
\end{example}

\Note
In the first test case, one valid arrangement is:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|} \hline
    0 & 1 \\ \hline    
    2 & 3 \\ \hline
\end{tabular} 
\end{center}

There are $9$ subgrids in total, and the $4$ of them with non-zero MEX are shown below:


\begin{center}

\begin{tabular}{|c|} \hline
    0 \\ \hline   
\end{tabular} \small{values:\it{$[0]$}~--- MEX: \it{$1$}}


\begin{tabular}{|c|c|} \hline
    0 & 1 \\ \hline    
\end{tabular}  \small{values:\it{$[0, 1]$ }~--- MEX: \it{$2$}}


\begin{tabular}{|c|} \hline
    0 \\ \hline    
    2 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}  \small{values:\it{$[0, 2]$ }~--- MEX: \it{$1$}}


\begin{tabular}{|c|c|} \hline
    0 & 1 \\ \hline    
    2 & 3 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}  \small{values:\it{$[0, 1, 2, 3]$ }~--- MEX: \it{$4$}}

\end{center}

The sum of MEX over all subgrids would be $1+2+1+4 = 8$. 
It can be proven that no other arrangements have a larger sum of MEX values.

\end{problem}

", "examples": {"example.01.a": "0 1 
2 3 
8 4 5 
6 0 1 
7 2 3", "example.01": "2
2
3
"}}

{"contestId": 2102, "index": "E", "name": "23 Kingdom", "statement": "\begin{problem}{23 Kingdom}{standard input}{standard output}{4 seconds}{256 megabytes}

\NewDocumentCommand{\DefTestcases}{O{t}O{10^4}}{Each test contains multiple test cases.
The first line contains the number of test cases $#1$ ($1 \le #1 \le #2$).
The description of the test cases follows.
}
\NewDocumentCommand{\DefLimitSum}{O{n}O{10^5}}{It is guaranteed that the sum of $#1$ over all test cases does not exceed $#2$.
}

The \textit{distance} of a value $x$ in an array $c$, denoted as $d_x(c)$, is defined as the largest gap between any two occurrences of $x$ in $c$. 

Formally, $d_x(c) = \max(j - i)$ over all pairs $i < j$ where $c_i = c_j = x$. If $x$ appears only once or not at all in $c$, then $d_x(c) = 0$. 

The \textit{beauty} of an array is the sum of the distances of each distinct value in the array. Formally, the beauty of an array $c$ is equal to $\sum\limits_{1\le x\le n} d_x(c)$.

Given an array $a$ of length $n$, an array $b$ is \textit{nice} if it also has length $n$ and its elements satisfy $1\le b_i\le a_i$ for all $1\le i\le n$. Your task is to find the maximum possible beauty of any nice array.

\InputFile
\DefTestcases[t][10^4]

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $n$ ($1\le n\le 2\cdot10^5$)~--- the length of array $a$.

The second line of each test case contains $n$ integers $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$ ($1\le a_i\le n$)~--- the elements of array $a$.

\DefLimitSum[n][2\cdot10^5]

\OutputFile
For each test case, output a single integer representing the maximum possible beauty among all nice arrays.

\Example

\begin{example}
\exmpfile{example.01}{example.01.a}%
\end{example}

\Note
In the first test case, if $b = [1, 2, 1, 2]$, then $d_1(b) = 3 - 1 = 2$ and $d_2(b) = 4 - 2 = 2$, resulting in a beauty of $2 + 2 = 4$. It can be proven that there are no nice arrays with a beauty greater than $4$.

In the second test case, both $b = [1, 1]$ and $b = [2, 2]$ are valid solutions with a beauty of $1$.

In the third test case, if $b = [1, 2, 1, 4, 1, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2]$ with $d_1(b) = 9 - 1 = 8$, $d_2(b) = 10 - 2 = 8$, and $d_4(b) = 0$, resulting in a beauty of $16$.

\end{problem}

", "examples": {"example.01.a": "4
1
16
16
", "example.01": "4
4
1 2 1 2
2
2 2
10
1 2 1 5 1 2 2 1 1 2
8
1 5 2 8 4 1 4 2
"}}
